refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall achieve and maintain operational availability of ≥0.97 (97%) over the agreed evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the system is available for productive operation to total scheduled operational time, accounting for planned maintenance, unplanned downtime, and safety-driven shutdowns.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-004 is a key performance indicator (KPI) that directly impacts customer satisfaction and mission success. Refining it requires explicit definition of: (1) the measurement period and baseline, (2) what constitutes 'available' vs. 'unavailable' (e.g., does safety shutdown count as unavailability?), (3) which subsystems or failure modes dominate unavailability, and (4) the allocation of availability targets across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services. High safety criticality means that safety-driven shutdowns must be distinguished from reliability failures.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Collect operational telemetry (uptime/downtime logs, failure modes, maintenance events) over the agreed evaluation period. Calculate availability as (Total Time − Unplanned Downtime − Planned Maintenance) / Total Time. Compare against the ≥0.97 threshold. Stratify by subsystem (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) to identify bottlenecks.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The Vehicle subsystem shall achieve ≥0.98 availability (allocated target).
FR-NGHE-004.2: The Teleoperation subsystem shall achieve ≥0.99 availability (allocated target).
FR-NGHE-004.3: The Safety Services subsystem shall achieve ≥0.99 availability (allocated target).
FR-NGHE-004.4: The system shall provide real-time availability monitoring and alerting to the Site Manager and Maintenance team.
FR-NGHE-004.5: The system shall support predictive maintenance scheduling to minimize unplanned downtime.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement redundancy (dual motors, dual communication links, dual safety controllers) and fault detection/isolation/recovery (FDIR) logic across all critical subsystems.
DP-B: Establish a preventive maintenance schedule based on component mean-time-between-failure (MTBF) data and condition monitoring, with spare parts inventory management.
DP-C: Design the system architecture to minimize single points of failure (SPOF) through modular design, hot-swappable components, and graceful degradation modes.
DP-D: Implement a comprehensive availability monitoring and predictive analytics platform that tracks subsystem health, predicts failures, and recommends maintenance actions.
DP-E: Combine DP-A, DP-B, DP-C, and DP-D into an integrated availability assurance strategy.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-E: Integrated Availability Assurance Strategy combining redundancy/FDIR (DP-A), preventive maintenance scheduling (DP-B), SPOF elimination via modular design (DP-C), and real-time monitoring/predictive analytics (DP-D). This multi-faceted approach addresses both hardware reliability and operational practices, necessary to achieve ≥0.97 availability in a safety-critical heavy equipment system.

potential_sources: Redundancy (DP-A) may increase system complexity and cost, potentially conflicting with maintainability (DP-B).
Modular design (DP-C) requires clear interface definitions; poor interface design could negate redundancy benefits.
Monitoring/analytics platform (DP-D) depends on reliable telemetry from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems; if telemetry is unavailable, predictive maintenance cannot function.
coupling_check.notes: The selected DP-E is a composite strategy. Coupling is manageable if: (1) redundancy is applied selectively to high-FMEA-risk components, (2) modular design enforces strict interface contracts, and (3) the monitoring platform has its own availability target (≥0.99). These are design trade-offs, not fundamental contradictions.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Redundancy can be cost-effectively implemented without exceeding project budget constraints.
Assumption: Spare parts supply chain is reliable and lead times are acceptable.
Assumption: Operators and maintenance teams will follow preventive maintenance schedules.
Risk: Over-reliance on predictive analytics; if the analytics model is inaccurate, maintenance may be scheduled incorrectly, increasing downtime.
Risk: Redundancy introduces additional failure modes (e.g., cross-coupling between redundant channels); FDIR logic must be thoroughly validated.
Risk: Safety-driven shutdowns (e.g., emergency stop) may be counted as unavailability by the customer, reducing measured availability even if the system is functioning correctly.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Clarify with the customer: (1) Does the ≥0.97 availability target include or exclude planned maintenance windows and safety-driven shutdowns? (2) What is the acceptable cost premium for redundancy? (3) What is the expected MTBF for critical components (e.g., motor, hydraulic pump, communication module)? Use this data to allocate availability targets to subsystems and justify the redundancy strategy.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: The NGHE Solution is the top-level system integration module responsible for orchestrating availability across all subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services). It owns the availability monitoring platform, predictive maintenance logic, and system-level FDIR coordination. It does not own the detailed implementation of Vehicle propulsion, Teleoperation control algorithms, or Safety Services logic, but it integrates their telemetry and health status to compute and maintain the overall availability KPI.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Inbound telemetry from Vehicle (motor status, hydraulic pressure, fuel level, odometer), Teleoperation (link quality, latency, command queue depth), and Safety Services (sensor readings, alert status). Used to compute subsystem health and predict failures.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Outbound tasking to Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems to execute maintenance actions (e.g., reduce load, return to depot for service) or graceful degradation (e.g., switch to backup motor). Also used to schedule preventive maintenance windows.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional safety alerts: inbound from Safety Services (e.g., emergency stop triggered), outbound to Safety Services (e.g., availability status for safety decision logic). Ensures that safety-driven shutdowns are properly logged and distinguished from reliability failures.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Inbound network connectivity status from Teleoperation subsystem. Used to assess availability of remote monitoring and tele-operation capabilities.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the natural owner of system-level availability orchestration, monitoring, and predictive analytics. It has clear ownership of the availability KPI and integrates telemetry from all subsystems. Minimal diffusion: only the detailed FDIR logic for individual subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) remains distributed.

Vehicle owns its own propulsion and hydraulic subsystems, which are major contributors to availability. However, Vehicle should not own the system-level availability monitoring or predictive analytics platform; these are cross-cutting concerns. Assigning DP-E to Vehicle would create diffusion of the monitoring platform across multiple subsystems.

Teleoperation owns communication link redundancy and FDIR for remote control. However, it should not own the system-level availability KPI or predictive maintenance scheduling, which span all subsystems. Assigning DP-E to Teleoperation would fragment the availability strategy.

Safety Services owns safety-critical shutdown logic and sensor monitoring. However, it should not own the availability KPI or predictive maintenance, which are operational concerns distinct from safety. Assigning DP-E to Safety Services would conflate safety and availability, creating confusion about what constitutes a 'failure' vs. a 'safe shutdown'.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The NGHE Solution module is the appropriate owner of DP-E (Integrated Availability Assurance Strategy) because: (1) it has the broadest visibility into all subsystems via Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, and Network Link interfaces; (2) it can coordinate redundancy, FDIR, and maintenance scheduling across subsystems without creating diffusion; (3) it can distinguish safety-driven shutdowns (SafetyAlert) from reliability failures (Telemetry), which is critical for accurate availability measurement; (4) it owns the availability KPI and is accountable for meeting the ≥0.97 target. Subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) own their own internal reliability and FDIR, but NGHE Solution orchestrates the system-level strategy.
containment_risks: Risk: NGHE Solution may become a 'god module' if it tries to own too much detailed logic. Mitigation: Clearly separate system-level orchestration (owned by NGHE Solution) from subsystem-level FDIR (owned by Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services).

Risk: Predictive analytics platform may require real-time processing and low-latency decision-making. If NGHE Solution is implemented as a centralized software service, network latency or service unavailability could degrade the availability assurance itself. Mitigation: Implement the monitoring platform with redundancy and edge computing (e.g., local health monitoring on Vehicle, with cloud-based analytics for long-term trend analysis).

Risk: Telemetry interface may be noisy or unreliable, leading to false positives in failure prediction. Mitigation: Implement data validation and filtering logic in NGHE Solution to ensure high confidence in predictive alerts before triggering maintenance actions.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Refine the NGHE Solution module boundary to explicitly separate: (1) System-level availability monitoring and KPI computation (owned by NGHE Solution), (2) Predictive analytics and maintenance scheduling (owned by NGHE Solution or a dedicated Analytics subsystem), (3) Subsystem-level FDIR and redundancy (owned by Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services). Define clear handoff points and interface contracts between these layers. Consider whether the predictive analytics platform should be implemented as a separate module (e.g., 'Availability Analytics') to avoid overloading NGHE Solution.

fr_dp_gate.reason: The refined FR (achieve ≥0.97 availability over the evaluation period) is directly addressed by the selected DP-E (Integrated Availability Assurance Strategy). DP-E combines redundancy, preventive maintenance, modular design, and monitoring—all necessary to achieve the availability target. The FR is measurable (availability ratio), and the DP is implementable. No fundamental mismatch.

diffusion_risks: Diffusion Risk 1: If redundancy logic (DP-A) is implemented separately in Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services without coordination, the system-level FDIR may not function correctly. Mitigation: NGHE Solution must own the coordination logic and define clear FDIR protocols for each subsystem.

Diffusion Risk 2: If predictive analytics (DP-D) is implemented as a separate cloud service outside the NGHE Solution module, there is a risk of data silos and delayed feedback. Mitigation: Ensure the analytics platform is tightly integrated with NGHE Solution's monitoring interface (Telemetry).

Diffusion Risk 3: Preventive maintenance scheduling (DP-B) may be owned by Maintenance/Quality team (external to NGHE Solution). Mitigation: Define a clear interface (e.g., Tasking) for NGHE Solution to communicate maintenance recommendations to the Maintenance team.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the appropriate IM boundary for DP-E. It has high containment (0.95) and low diffusion (0.05). The main diffusion is the delegation of subsystem-level FDIR to Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services, which is intentional and manageable via interface contracts.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of FR-NGHE-004 (≥0.97 availability) is feasible via operational telemetry collection and post-hoc analysis. NGHE Solution's Telemetry interface provides the necessary data (uptime/downtime logs, failure modes). Verification requires: (1) agreement with customer on evaluation period and what counts as 'unavailable', (2) instrumentation of all subsystems to report health status, (3) a data collection and analysis pipeline (owned by NGHE Solution or Analytics subsystem).
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: The allocation of availability targets to subsystems (FR-NGHE-004.1 to FR-NGHE-004.3) is clear: Vehicle ≥0.98, Teleoperation ≥0.99, Safety Services ≥0.99. These targets are derived from a simple series model (overall availability ≈ 0.98 × 0.99 × 0.99 ≈ 0.96, which is slightly below the ≥0.97 target; this should be revisited in the next decomposition level). The allocation is traceable to the top-level FR and can be verified at the subsystem level.

recommended_revisions: Revision 1: Clarify with the customer whether the ≥0.97 availability target includes or excludes planned maintenance and safety-driven shutdowns. This will affect how availability is measured and reported.

Revision 2: Revisit the subsystem availability allocations (FR-NGHE-004.1 to FR-NGHE-004.3). The current allocation (0.98 × 0.99 × 0.99 ≈ 0.96) is slightly below the ≥0.97 target. Consider increasing the Vehicle target to ≥0.985 or adding a margin for other failure modes.

Revision 3: Define the detailed FDIR protocols and redundancy strategies for each subsystem (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) in the next decomposition level. Ensure that NGHE Solution's coordination logic is compatible with subsystem-level FDIR.

Revision 4: Specify the data collection, storage, and analysis infrastructure for the monitoring platform (DP-D). Decide whether this should be implemented as a separate Analytics module or integrated into NGHE Solution.

decision.justification: The refined FR-NGHE-004 and selected DP-E are sound and well-aligned. The assigned IM (NGHE Solution) is appropriate and has high containment. However, the FR is not atomic—it has been decomposed into five sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-004.1 to FR-NGHE-004.5), each of which requires further decomposition to define concrete design parameters and implementation modules. Additionally, the subsystem availability allocations (0.98 × 0.99 × 0.99 ≈ 0.96) need to be revisited to ensure they meet the ≥0.97 target. Proceeding to the next decomposition level is necessary to detail the redundancy strategies, maintenance scheduling logic, and monitoring platform architecture.
decision.justification_detail: This is a KPI-level requirement that spans multiple subsystems and operational practices. A single decomposition level is insufficient to capture the full design intent. The next level should decompose each sub-FR (FR-NGHE-004.1 to FR-NGHE-004.5) into concrete DPs and IMs, such as: (1) Motor redundancy and switchover logic (DP for FR-NGHE-004.1), (2) Communication link redundancy and failover (DP for FR-NGHE-004.2), (3) Safety sensor redundancy and voting (DP for FR-NGHE-004.3), (4) Telemetry collection and health status computation (DP for FR-NGHE-004.4), (5) Predictive maintenance algorithms and scheduling (DP for FR-NGHE-004.5).
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The Vehicle subsystem shall achieve ≥0.98 availability through redundant propulsion (dual motors with automatic switchover) and condition monitoring of hydraulic and fuel systems.
FR-NGHE-004.2: The Teleoperation subsystem shall achieve ≥0.99 availability through redundant communication links (primary and backup networks) and automatic failover.

FR-NGHE-004.3: The Safety Services subsystem shall achieve ≥0.99 availability through redundant safety sensors and voting logic, with graceful degradation to reduced functionality if one sensor fails.

FR-NGHE-004.4: The NGHE Solution shall provide real-time availability monitoring by collecting telemetry from all subsystems, computing health status, and alerting the Site Manager and Maintenance team to degraded conditions.

FR-NGHE-004.5: The NGHE Solution shall support predictive maintenance by analyzing historical telemetry data, predicting component failures, and recommending maintenance actions to minimize unplanned downtime.

assumptions: Assumption 1: The customer's ≥0.97 availability target is measured over a 12-month operational period, excluding planned maintenance windows and safety-driven shutdowns.
Assumption 2: Redundancy can be cost-effectively implemented for critical components (motors, communication links, safety sensors) without exceeding project budget.
Assumption 3: Spare parts supply chain is reliable with acceptable lead times (e.g., ≤2 weeks for critical components).
Assumption 4: Operators and maintenance teams will follow preventive maintenance schedules and use the predictive maintenance recommendations.
Assumption 5: Telemetry data from Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services is reliable and available with ≥99% uptime.
Assumption 6: The NGHE Solution can be implemented with sufficient computational capacity to perform real-time monitoring and predictive analytics without introducing latency or availability issues.
open_risks: Risk 1: Subsystem availability allocations (0.98 × 0.99 × 0.99 ≈ 0.96) are slightly below the ≥0.97 target. Need to revisit allocations or add margin for other failure modes.
Risk 2: Redundancy introduces additional failure modes (e.g., cross-coupling between redundant channels). FDIR logic must be thoroughly validated to avoid introducing new failure paths.

Risk 3: Predictive analytics model may be inaccurate, leading to false positives (unnecessary maintenance) or false negatives (missed failures). Model validation and continuous improvement are required.
Risk 4: Safety-driven shutdowns may be counted as unavailability by the customer, reducing measured availability even if the system is functioning correctly. Clarification with customer is needed.

Risk 5: Teleoperation link unavailability may be caused by external factors (e.g., network outages) beyond the control of the NGHE Solution. Mitigation strategy (e.g., autonomous operation mode) may be needed.
Risk 6: Maintenance team may not have sufficient training or resources to execute preventive maintenance recommendations, leading to missed maintenance and increased downtime.
evidence_needed: Evidence 1: Customer confirmation of the ≥0.97 availability target definition (measurement period, what counts as unavailable, acceptable exclusions).
Evidence 2: MTBF and MTTR data for critical components (motors, hydraulic pumps, communication modules, safety sensors) to justify redundancy and maintenance scheduling strategies.
Evidence 3: Cost-benefit analysis of redundancy options (e.g., dual motors vs. single motor with faster repair) to ensure cost-effectiveness.
Evidence 4: Detailed FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis) for Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services subsystems to identify high-risk failure modes and justify redundancy allocation.
Evidence 5: Validation plan for predictive analytics model, including historical data analysis, model accuracy metrics, and continuous improvement process.
Evidence 6: Operational procedures and training plan for Maintenance team to ensure compliance with preventive maintenance schedules.
Evidence 7: Network reliability analysis for Teleoperation link, including redundancy options and autonomous operation fallback strategies.
